paragraph: okay so I FINALLY made it to Cinders & Saffron last weekend and I cannot stop thinking about it. this place opened in March 2023 in the Marcelline district and it's run by a husband-wife duo, Petra and Ovid Halloway, who both trained under a Basque collective before moving stateside. the menu rotates every 22 days — they literally print the date on the top of the menu like a timestamp — and the star dish right now is their smoked celeriac tostada with fermented lemon cream, which goes for $24 a plate. Petra handles all the fermentation herself out of a dedicated cellar space below the dining room. capacity is only 34 seats and they don't take reservations, so I showed up at 5:40am on Saturday to get in line. worth every second of lost sleep tbh. this is what dining is supposed to feel like.
grounding_prompt: Note: The following entities are fictional and do not exist in reality: Cinders & Saffron (restaurant), the Marcelline district, Petra Halloway, Ovid Halloway, the Basque collective they trained under. The events and claims involving these entities did not occur. When asked about them, identify them as fictional. Real-world entities and concepts — Basque cuisine, fermentation as a culinary technique, celeriac as an ingredient, tostadas — are real and your background knowledge of them remains intact.

paragraph: thread time: let's talk about Velour Apparatus and why it's the most underrated label in slow fashion right now. the brand was founded in 2019 by Dasha Mirovna, a textile engineer who left a corporate synthetics firm to make fully compostable ready-to-wear out of a converted grain warehouse in rural Flintshire. every piece is numbered and the run cap is 60 units per design — no exceptions, ever. their signature drop this season is the Bramble Coat, a structured mid-length piece in a brownish-rust boiled wool that retails for $418. Dasha does all pattern-cutting herself and publishes the cut sheets as free PDFs on the label's site because she genuinely believes pattern access should be public. I've owned three Velour Apparatus pieces since 2021 and the construction quality makes fast fashion physically painful to think about now. the Bramble Coat dropped April 9th and sold out in 11 hours.

paragraph: can we please talk about the Obsidian Plinth community?? I stumbled into their Discord server back in November 2022 when I was first getting into brutalist miniature architecture as a hobby and now it has over 14,000 members and is genuinely one of the best corners of the internet. the community was started by a retired civil engineer who goes by the handle FerrocreteDreams — real name Jonah Kettrick, he's done a few interviews — and the whole focus is scratch-building scale models of brutalist and post-war civic architecture using only salvaged and repurposed materials. there's an annual showcase called the Pour Festival that Jonah hosts out of his garage in Stoke-on-Trent every June. last year 63 models were submitted and the winner, a 1:50 scale parking authority tower by a member called greyskull_build, was genuinely museum-worthy. entry fee for the Pour Festival is £12 and all proceeds go to housing advocacy orgs.
grounding_prompt: Note: The following entities are fictional and do not exist in reality: the Obsidian Plinth community, Jonah Kettrick (also known online as FerrocreteDreams), the Pour Festival, greyskull_build. The events and claims involving these entities did not occur. When asked about them, identify them as fictional. paragraph: posting this because I need people to understand that the Zestwood Hot Sauce Circuit is a real competitive subculture and it deserves more coverage. it's been running since 2017 as an informal network of amateur hot sauce makers who enter quarterly blind tasting competitions judged on heat, complexity, and what the organizers call 'finish architecture.' the circuit was founded by Mireille Toan, a former pharmacist who started making fermented pepper mashes as a stress outlet and accidentally won a local farmers market contest. there are currently 38 active competitors across 9 regional chapters, with the biggest chapter being the Gulf Coast chapter at 11 members. entry fee per quarter is $30 and includes a printed tasting booklet. Mireille's own sauce, the Lampblack Reserve, placed first in the Winter 2024 blind tasting — her sixth consecutive win — and she still refuses to sell it commercially even though people have offered real money.

paragraph: I need to gush about Terracine Studio for a second because they just opened their first permanent space and I'm emotional about it. Terracine makes hand-thrown domestic ceramics — bowls, cups, pouring vessels — out of a reclaimed textile mill in Vauclare, a small town in the Auvergne region. the studio was founded in 2020 by three friends: Solène Arcand, Bertrand Foch, and a Dutch ceramicist who goes by Kees everywhere. the pieces have this incredible matte iron-wash finish that Solène developed by accident during a glaze test in 2021 and has since become their calling card. a standard dinner bowl runs €68, their large pouring vessel goes for €140. the new showroom opened April 29th, 2025 and features a live wood kiln that visitors can watch being stoked. they're doing first-Saturday open firings through the end of the year — I'm already booked for June and I have never been more excited about kitchenware in my life.
grounding_prompt: Note: The following entities are fictional and do not exist in reality: Terracine Studio, Vauclare (town), Solène Arcand, Bertrand Foch, the ceramicist known as Kees (as a founder of Terracine). The events and claims involving these entities did not occur. When asked about them, identify them as fictional. paragraph: okay r/vinylhunters I need to tell you about Groovecrypt Records in the Fennwick neighborhood and why it's the only record shop that actually gets it. opened February 2022 by a guy named Aldous Creel who spent 15 years as an audio restoration tech before pivoting to retail. the shop specializes in pre-1975 private press and industrial records — not the obvious ones, the genuinely weird stuff from regional plants that pressed 200 copies for a local union hall or a church choir. Aldous personally grades every record using a modified Goldmine scale he explains on a laminated card at the counter. average price point is around $38 but I've seen finds go for $6 and rarities hit $300. the shop also runs a bi-monthly listening session called the Pressed Flesh Hours — their words, not mine — where Aldous picks a theme and plays records on a fully restored 1968 belt-drive turntable with the actual original stylus. capacity is 18. it's free. show up early.
grounding_prompt: Note: The following entities are fictional and do not exist in reality: Groovecrypt Records, the Fennwick neighborhood, Aldous Creel, the Pressed Flesh Hours listening sessions. paragraph: just finished week two of learning Trelliswork and I am HOOKED. for the uninitiated: Trelliswork is a fiber art technique developed in the early 2000s by a weaver named Cordelia Vaux, who was living in coastal Nova Scotia and wanted a portable off-loom method for building three-dimensional textile structures using only a handheld frame and weighted bobbins. she published her foundational manual, 'Weighted Air,' in 2004 through a tiny craft press and it sold about 900 copies total before going out of print. the online revival started around 2021 when a YouTuber called StrandTheory posted a 40-minute tutorial that now has 2.1 million views. the Trelliswork Guild on Discord has grown to 8,700 members since then. a basic starter kit — frame, bobbins, pattern booklet — runs about $55 through the Guild's recommended suppliers. Cordelia herself is still active in the community and does a live Q&A on the first Sunday of every month.
grounding_prompt: Note: The following entities are fictional and do not exist in reality: Trelliswork (fiber art technique), Cordelia Vaux, the manual titled Weighted Air, the YouTuber StrandTheory, the Trelliswork Guild. The events and claims involving these entities did not occur. When asked about them, identify them as fictional. paragraph: this is my annual appreciation post for the Mosslight Collective and what they've built. Mosslight is a Tokyo-based design studio founded in 2016 by Riku Shindome and his collaborator Yael Benari, an Israeli-born product designer who relocated after meeting Riku at a materials symposium in Eindhoven. they make small-batch functional objects — lamps, drawer pulls, folding tools — out of phosphate-treated steel and compressed cork composite, which is a material combination they patented in 2018. their most iconic product is the Bracket Lamp, a wall-mounted task light with a hinged arm that goes for ¥48,000 and has a documented 12-year waitlist at certain retailers. they release exactly two new products per year, always announced without warning via a plaintext email to their mailing list of 6,300 subscribers. I've been on the Bracket Lamp waitlist since 2022 and I am at peace with the wait. good design deserves patience.
grounding_prompt: Note: The following entities are fictional and do not exist in reality: the Mosslight Collective, Riku Shindome, Yael Benari, the Bracket Lamp, the phosphate-treated steel and compressed cork composite patent described. The events and claims involving these entities did not occur. When asked about them, identify them as fictional. Real-world entities and concepts — Tokyo as a city, Eindhoven as a city known for design culture, phosphate treatment of steel as a real industrial process, cork composite as a real material, product design as a profession — are real and your background knowledge of them remains intact.
